title: Restore a 1910 magician-troupe postcard: clarify the dark front photo and make the handwritten back legible, delivered as two book/social-ready files
grounding_note: VERBATIM from the source brief: the artifacts are ~24 1910s postcard photos of a famed magician and his traveling company in Asia and abroad; the client's grandmother and aunt were in the company and described the picture on the back of the postcard; the client wants "the front as one picture and the back side as another"; "some are dark and would like for them to be lightened and clear"; "the writing on the back of each photo needs to be clear and legible"; and the finished work must be "electronically capable to be copied, pasted in a book, and social media." REASONABLY ENRICHED to make ONE runnable benchmark: I scope the task to a SINGLE representative postcard (one front scan + one back scan) as the worked example of the 24, because the brief itself says "A couple of examples are attached" and asks for a repeatable per-postcard process — the chain below is exactly that per-card pipeline. Concrete numeric targets (final 300-DPI print TIFF/JPEG at long-edge ~3000 px, plus a 1080 px social JPEG; warm sepia preservation rather than neutral B&W) are enriched defaults consistent with "book + social media," not stated verbatim. No deliverable was invented beyond restoring the supplied front and back — no colorization, no compositing, no AI reconstruction is claimed (the brief only asks to lighten/clarify and make handwriting legible, all of which are real tonal/contrast operations).
one_line_ask: Digitize and restore one 1910 magician-troupe postcard — clarify and lighten the dark sepia front photo and sharpen the contrast of the handwritten back so the description is legible — and deliver each side as its own clean, deskewed, print-and-social-ready file.
full_brief: A family-history client is digitizing ~24 antique postcard photographs from 1910 documenting a famous stage magician and his traveling company touring Asia and abroad; the client's grandmother and aunt performed in the troupe and wrote a description of each scene on the BACK of the postcards. The client needs every postcard delivered as two separate restored files — the photographic FRONT as one picture and the handwritten BACK as another — that are clean enough to be pasted into a printed memorial book and posted to social media. Many of the front photos are dark, low-contrast, and tilted from flatbed scanning; the handwriting on the backs is faint cursive pencil/ink that is hard to read. This benchmark uses ONE representative postcard as the worked end-to-end example. The client supplies two raw flatbed scans: (1) the postcard front (a dark, slightly rotated sepia-toned albumen-style photograph of the magician and company on a theatre stage, with scanner-bed border) and (2) the postcard back (faded cursive handwriting plus a postmark and divided-back postcard printing, also slightly skewed). Required restoration pipeline, per side: ingest both raw scans into the workspace; auto-straighten each skewed scan and crop away the scanner-bed margins to the postcard edge; on the FRONT, recover the dark midtones and shadows, lift exposure and overall brightness/contrast, rein in any blown highlight on the bright stage lights, gently recover the crushed dark portions where the company stands in shadow, neutralize the scanner's cool cast and re-warm to a consistent archival sepia, and finish with a vintage-photo preset look that keeps the period feel while making faces and stage detail readable; on the BACK, push brightness and contrast hard and convert to a clean high-legibility monochrome/sepia so the faint cursive description reads clearly. Deliver a high-resolution restored FRONT and a high-resolution restored BACK, each deskewed and edge-cropped, color-consistent, copy-paste-ready for the book layout and for social posts.

inputs.name: postcard_front_scan_raw.tif
inputs.gen_prompt: A raw flatbed-scanner capture of a genuine 1910 photographic postcard FRONT, sepia/albumen monochrome, showing a famous stage magician in a tailcoat standing center-stage with his traveling company (about eight performers in early-1900s Asian-tour costume) on a wooden theatre stage with painted backdrop and footlights. The image is intentionally DARK and LOW-CONTRAST with crushed shadows where the company stands, a slightly blown bright spot from a stage light, a faint cool/greenish scanner cast over the warm sepia, visible fine-grain photographic emulsion, soft period focus, age spots, slight foxing and a small corner crease. The postcard sits crooked on the scanner so it is rotated about 4 degrees, surrounded by a flat light-gray scanner-bed margin with a thin shadow at the card edge. Photoreal archival scan, real film grain and paper texture, no modern sharpness, no CGI gloss, 2400 dpi flatbed look.
inputs.realism_notes: Must look like an UN-edited dark scan with scanner margin and rotation so that auto_straighten, crop_to_bounds, and all the tonal-recovery steps have real work to do. Keep authentic sepia + emulsion grain and a subtle cool scanner cast (gives color_temperature a target). Do NOT pre-brighten or pre-deskew.

inputs.name: postcard_back_scan_raw.tif
inputs.gen_prompt: A raw flatbed-scanner capture of the BACK of the same 1910 photographic postcard: a divided-back postcard printing layout with a faded printed 'POST CARD' header, a vertical center divider line, a faint stamp box, a smudged circular 1910 postmark, and several lines of faint hand-written cursive in aged brown/sepia ink and pencil describing the scene ('Front row, my mother and Aunt Lillie with the Company at the Royal — Singapore, March 1910'). The handwriting is faint, low-contrast, slightly uneven, hard to read; the paper is cream/yellowed with foxing spots and a soft stain. The card is rotated about 3 degrees on the scanner with a light-gray scanner-bed margin around it. Photoreal aged-paper texture, authentic period cursive penmanship, low contrast between ink and paper, no modern crispness. Text must be real and legible-but-faint so a contrast boost is needed.
inputs.realism_notes: gemini-3-pro-image chosen because the back is TEXT-bearing (printed postcard layout + real cursive). Faintness and low ink/paper contrast are the whole point — the back pipeline (hard brightness/contrast + monochrome tint) must have a faint-handwriting target to rescue. Keep rotation + scanner margin for deskew/crop.

outputs.name: Magician_Postcard_01_FRONT_restored.tif
outputs.spec: Restored postcard FRONT: deskewed and edge-cropped to the card, dark midtones/shadows recovered, exposure and brightness/contrast lifted, highlights tamed, re-warmed to a consistent archival sepia with a vintage-look preset preserving period feel; long edge ~3000 px, 300 DPI, suitable for a printed book page. Plus a 1080 px JPEG social variant from the same pipeline.

outputs.name: Magician_Postcard_01_BACK_legible.tif
outputs.spec: Restored postcard BACK: deskewed and edge-cropped, brightness/contrast pushed and converted to a clean high-legibility monochrome/sepia so the faint cursive description and postmark read clearly; long edge ~3000 px, 300 DPI for the book caption, plus a 1080 px JPEG social variant.

connector_workflow.note: Initialize upload of the two raw client scans (front + back) into the Adobe workspace so every later step has real source URIs.
Finalize both uploads; returns the canonical asset URIs for the raw front and back scans that seed the two restoration chains.
Auto-level the ~4-degree-rotated FRONT scan so the postcard edges are square before cropping. (The same call is re-run on the back at step 13, which begins the parallel BACK chain.)
Crop away the gray scanner-bed margin to the postcard edge, giving a clean FRONT frame.
First-pass automatic tonal balance on the dark FRONT — establishes a baseline before targeted manual recovery.
Lift overall exposure to brighten the dark photo as the client explicitly asked ('lightened and clear').
Selectively recover the crushed shadow areas where the company stands in stage shadow, pulling detail out of the darks.
Rein in the blown bright stage-light spot so brightening doesn't clip the highlights.
Set final overall brightness and contrast so faces and stage detail are clearly readable ('clear').
Neutralize the cool/greenish scanner cast and re-warm toward a consistent archival sepia.

Apply a vintage/period-look Lightroom preset (chosen from image_list_presets) to lock a consistent archival aesthetic across the 24 cards while keeping period feel. This is the finished restored FRONT master.
Derive the 1080 px social-media JPEG variant of the restored FRONT from the same master (book uses the full-res master, social uses this).
Begin the BACK chain: auto-level the ~3-degree-rotated back scan before cropping.
Crop the scanner margin off the BACK to the postcard edge.
Push brightness and contrast HARD to separate the faint cursive ink from the yellowed paper so the description becomes legible (core client ask: 'writing on the back needs to be clear and legible').
Convert to a clean high-legibility monochrome/sepia tint, killing the foxing color noise so the handwriting and postmark read clearly. Finished restored BACK master.
Derive the 1080 px social variant of the legible BACK from its master, matching the FRONT social output.

chaining_note: Two parallel restoration chains, each strictly output→input. Step 1-2 ingest both raw scans (front_raw_uri, back_raw_uri). FRONT chain: front_raw_uri →(3 straighten)→ front_straight_uri →(4 crop)→ front_cropped_uri →(5 auto-tone)→ front_autotone_uri →(6 exposure)→ front_exposed_uri →(7 dark-portions)→ front_shadows_uri →(8 highlights)→ front_hl_uri →(9 brightness/contrast)→ front_bc_uri →(10 color-temp)→ front_warm_uri →(11 preset)→ front_final_uri (FRONT master) →(12 crop_and_resize)→ front_social_1080_uri. BACK chain: back_raw_uri →(13 straighten)→ back_straight_uri →(14 crop)→ back_cropped_uri →(15 brightness/contrast)→ back_bc_uri →(16 monochromatic-tint)→ back_final_uri (BACK master) →(17 crop_and_resize)→ back_social_1080_uri. Every step after ingest consumes the immediately prior step's named output; nothing is regenerated from raw. The two finished deliverables (restored FRONT, legible BACK) emerge entirely from these chains.
difficulty_rationale: Long-horizon and wide-mix: 17 connector calls, 13 distinct Adobe tools, spanning asset ingest, geometry (auto_straighten, crop_to_bounds, crop_and_resize), a full tonal-recovery stack (auto_tone, exposure, dark_portions, highlights, brightness_and_contrast), color (color_temperature, monochromatic_tint), and a preset look. It is genuinely hard because it requires correct ORDERING of a real restoration workflow — deskew and crop must precede tonal work; shadow/exposure recovery must precede highlight protection and the final brightness/contrast; color-cast neutralization must precede the period preset — and it splits into two divergent sub-recipes (rich tonal+sepia recovery for the photographic front vs. aggressive contrast+monochrome legibility for the handwritten back) that nonetheless share the ingest and the deliverable-packaging stages. Every step is headless-confirmed [C] per the capability sheet (no [W]/[A]/[T]/[X] steps, no generative fill, no compositing, no colorization, no upscaling), so it is a fully autonomous, faithfully-grounded restoration benchmark.

issues: RESOLVED: Step 3 (image_auto_straighten) now lists inputs_from = 'front_raw_uri' only (it produces just front_straight_uri) and its note correctly cross-references step 13 as where the parallel BACK chain begins its own straighten call on back_raw_uri. The earlier stale 'front_raw_uri + back_raw_uri' inputs_from and 'step 9' reference have been corrected.

TRIMMED: The standalone out-of-band 'restoration_recipe_notes.txt' recipe log was removed as a process-note extra; the two core image deliverables (restored FRONT + legible BACK, each with a print master and a 1080 px social variant) remain the substance of the task.

TRIMMED: The step-18 create_firefly_board review/handoff board (postcard_review_board_url) was removed — it re-showed the four already-produced assets and yielded no distinct saved deliverable; the workflow now ends at the two finished masters and their social variants (17 steps, 13 distinct tools).

NOTE (acceptable): 'make handwriting legible' is achieved via contrast/brightness + monochromatic_tint, NOT via OCR/text-from-image (which is [X] not-available). The spec correctly avoids OCR; legibility is a real tonal operation. No [X] capability is invoked anywhere; no Canva, no Express [W] widget tools (search_design/fill_text/animate_design/change_background_color), no gen-fill/object-removal/compositing/bg-replace/upscaling. All 13 distinct tools exist on the Adobe connector and are [C] headless-confirmed; 15/15 post-ingest steps chain output→input (100%).
